findings.summary: The verifier equation is implemented over public field elements, so the SRS directly reveals the trapdoor scalar as srs.powers[1]. For any commitment C, point z where z != s, and arbitrary claimed value y, an attacker can compute pi = (C - y) / (s - z) mod P and pass verify_eval(). This destroys KZG binding: commitments can be opened to values unrelated to the committed polynomial.
findings.evidence: Running the repro commits to f(x)=3x^3+2x^2+x+5, whose true value at z=7 is 1139, then forges a proof for y=123457928. verify_eval() returns True for the forged value.

findings.summary: commit() silently ignores polynomial coefficients whose indexes exceed len(srs.powers)-1 instead of rejecting polynomials above the SRS degree bound. As a result, distinct polynomials can have identical commitments while evaluating differently at the same point. A verifier can accept an opening for the truncated lower-degree polynomial as an opening of the too-high-degree polynomial's commitment.
findings.evidence: Running the repro with max_degree=4 shows commit(9) == commit(9 + x^5), while at z=2 the evaluations are 9 and 41. A proof for the value 9 verifies against the commitment returned for 9 + x^5.
